risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The women were randomised by SurveyMonkey, a web-based survey tool." Comment: This describes a web-based randomisation system used to assign participants, which is a proper random method and provides sufficient context beyond the word 'randomised' alone.

Quote: "The women were randomised by SurveyMonkey, a web-based survey tool." and "SurveyMonkey supplies a unique web address for all surveys." Comment: This indicates allocation was done through a web-based system rather than by an open list handled by recruiters, which reasonably prevents foreknowledge of the next assignment. No contradictory information suggests recruiters could peek at upcoming allocations.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A three-armed randomised controlled trial was conducted, comparing 12 sessions of traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) acupuncture in one group, 12 h of PFMT in a second group... and a waiting list control group" and "The women randomised to this group received no treatment". Comment: The trial does not report blinding of participants or the personnel delivering acupuncture/PFMT, and the interventions were obviously different and not masked. Because the main outcome was self-reported urinary incontinence symptoms (ICIQ-UI SF), knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence outcomes, so performance bias is high.

Quote: "ICIQ-UI SF is a well validated self-reporting outcome form" and "The second question concerning adverse events ... was included"; also "This trial could be biased by the fact that the acupuncturist and the investigator were the same person." Comment: The main clinical outcome was patient self-report, so the patient was the assessor. Because the study does not report participant blinding and the investigator/acupuncturist were the same person, knowledge of allocation could readily influence how symptoms and adverse events were reported. The outcome is subjective and therefore susceptible to detection bias.

Quote: "The number of dropouts was high in all groups: 50% in the waiting list group, 40% in the physiotherapy group, and 25% in the acupuncture group." Comment: Although attrition was not trivial, it was small and fairly balanced across the three arms in this pilot study, and the paper explains the main reasons (e.g. inconvenient training time, no effect, cancer, no interest in treatment) rather than suggesting outcome-related differential loss that would obviously bias the results. The authors also note that "once the women had started the interventions, compliance was high in the physiotherapy and acupuncture groups, and the questionnaires were completed properly," which supports that missing outcome data among completers was limited. Overall, the attrition pattern does not clearly indicate bias sufficient to rate this domain high.
